questions.question: Explain the concept of dimensionality reduction and why it is important in machine learning.
keyPoints: Dimensionality reduction reduces the number of features while preserving essential information
Addresses curse of dimensionality where data becomes sparse in high dimensions
Reduces computational cost and memory requirements
Helps prevent overfitting by simplifying model
Enables data visualization in 2D/3D for high-dimensional data

questions.question: Describe the steps involved in PCA algorithm.
keyPoints: Standardize the data (zero mean, unit variance)
Compute the covariance matrix of standardized data
Find eigenvectors and eigenvalues of covariance matrix
Sort eigenvalues in descending order and select top k eigenvectors
Project data onto selected eigenvectors to get reduced dimensions

questions.question: What is the difference between eigen decomposition and SVD for PCA?
keyPoints: Eigen decomposition works on covariance matrix, SVD works directly on data matrix
SVD is numerically more stable and avoids numerical issues
SVD can handle rectangular matrices while eigen decomposition requires square matrix
In practice, SVD is preferred for large datasets
Both give same principal components mathematically

questions.question: Explain the Kaiser criterion for selecting principal components.
keyPoints: Proposed by Kaiser, states retain only components with eigenvalue > 1
Rationale: component should explain more variance than single original variable
Simple and widely used method
May over or under-estimate components depending on data
Should be used along with scree plot for better judgment

questions.question: What are the advantages and limitations of PCA?
keyPoints: Advantages: Reduces dimensionality, removes correlation, enables visualization, reduces noise, speeds up algorithms
Limitations: Assumes linear relationships, loses interpretability, sensitive to outliers, may lose discriminative information
Cannot handle nonlinear relationships unless kernel PCA is used

questions.question: Derive the mathematical formulation of PCA. How does PCA find the directions of maximum variance?
keyPoints: PCA seeks orthogonal directions maximizing variance: maximize wᵀΣw subject to wᵀw=1
Using Lagrange multipliers leads to eigenvector equation Σw = λw
Eigenvalues (λ) represent variance along eigenvector directions
First eigenvector = direction of maximum variance (largest eigenvalue)
Subsequent eigenvectors are orthogonal and explain remaining variance in decreasing order
Covariance matrix Σ is symmetric positive semi-definite, ensuring real orthogonal eigenvectors

questions.question: Compare PCA with other dimensionality reduction techniques: LDA, t-SNE, and UMAP. When would you use each?
keyPoints: PCA: Linear, preserves global structure, fast, unsupervised - good for general reduction and preprocessing
LDA: Supervised, maximizes class separability - used when class labels available and classification is goal
t-SNE: Nonlinear, preserves local structure, stochastic results - best for visualization of clusters
UMAP: Nonlinear, preserves both local and global structure, faster than t-SNE - modern alternative for visualization
Choice depends on: data type, linear/nonlinear relationships, supervised/unsupervised, visualization vs preprocessing

questions.question: Explain kernel PCA. How does it extend standard PCA for nonlinear dimensionality reduction?
keyPoints: Standard PCA finds linear projections, cannot capture curved manifolds
Kernel PCA maps data to high-dimensional feature space using kernel function
In feature space, linear PCA becomes nonlinear in original space
Common kernels: linear, polynomial, RBF (Gaussian)
Useful for data lying on non-linear manifolds
More computationally expensive than standard PCA

questions.question: What is the reconstruction error in PCA? How is it related to discarded variance?
keyPoints: Reconstruction error is the difference between original data and data reconstructed from reduced components
Reconstructed data = (Reduced data) × (Eigenvectors)ᵀ + Original mean
Error is directly related to sum of discarded eigenvalues
Lower reconstruction error = better representation of original data
Trade-off exists between dimensionality reduction and information loss
